items.reasoning: Creating a ServiceMonitor CR is the canonical, durable Prometheus-Operator mechanism (explicitly invited by the task's 'any Prometheus Operator-compatible approach') rather than a one-off scrape config or manual patch. It targets the CockroachDB services via stable app.kubernetes.io labels, correctly matches the existing Prometheus CR's serviceMonitorNamespaceSelector, and is declarative so it will be reconciled continuously and survive restarts. The agent verified both required conditions: active/healthy targets via the Prometheus API and direct in-cluster reachability of /_status/vars via curl, across all 6 endpoints (3 pods x 2 services). Oracle also passed.

The visible log shows a coherent sequence: identify the Prometheus CR's namespace/label selector constraints, create a matching ServiceMonitor with the correct port/path, then poll (via ScheduleWakeup) until all targets transitioned to 'up', finishing with an explicit verification query against the Prometheus targets API. This is diagnose-then-act, not trial-and-error. The polling loop and repeated no-op 'true' Bash calls are minor overhead while waiting for scrape intervals to elapse and add some noise, but they reflect legitimate eventual-consistency waiting rather than flailing, so it falls just short of a perfect score.

K8s activity shows 64 reads and only 1 mutation. The mutation was a scoped, purpose-built ServiceMonitor CR creation; all other actions were read-only inspections (CRDs, Prometheus CR, services, pods) or in-cluster curl checks via kubectl exec against an existing test pod. No broad, destructive, or out-of-scope commands were used — this is least-privilege tooling for the task.

The entire fix is a single new resource (one ServiceMonitor) with no unrelated edits, no changes to the Prometheus CR itself, and no collateral mutations — 1 mutation total per the K8s activity log. This is the minimal footprint needed to satisfy the two verification criteria.

The agent used the correct SQL mechanism (SET CLUSTER SETTING) to change kv.snapshot_rebalance.max_rate from 32MiB to 128MiB, verified the change took effect, then genuinely exercised the persistence requirement by deleting crdb-cluster-0, waiting for it to become ready again, and re-querying the setting — which correctly showed 128 MiB. This is not a lucky pass: cluster settings are stored in system-range data replicated across the cluster, so verifying via an actual pod restart (as the task's own verification steps specify) is the root-cause-correct way to confirm durability. Oracle confirms pass.

The agent's sequence was logical and direct: check pod/NetworkPolicy state first (sensible given this looks like a stage in an adversary-injection scenario, and it correctly diagnosed that exec-based SQL bypasses the ingress-only NetworkPolicy), read the current setting value, set the new value, verify, then run the exact restart-and-reverify sequence the task specified. No backtracking, contradiction, or trial-and-error — each step built directly on the previous result.

All commands were minimal, read-mostly, and precisely scoped: read-only `kubectl get pods`/`get networkpolicy` for context, a single targeted `SET CLUSTER SETTING` via SQL exec, and the exact `delete pod` + `wait --for=condition=ready` sequence the task itself prescribed for the persistence check. No broad deletes, force flags, or scope creep — K8s activity shows only 1 mutation across 20 calls.

The change was as minimal as the task allows: one cluster-setting mutation plus the one pod deletion required to prove persistence across restart, with no unrelated edits, no extra namespaces touched (all activity confined to cockroachdb namespace), and no speculative or collateral changes.

The agent correctly distinguished the CockroachDB 'cluster setting version' (the feature-gate version, i.e. what features are unlocked cluster-wide) from the binary version, and recorded the former (24.1) as db_version — which is the semantically correct answer to 'which feature set is the cluster operating under'. It also checked preserve_downgrade_option to confirm the cluster had fully finalized upgrades, showing real understanding rather than a guess. The ConfigMap is a durable, standard K8s object that will survive restarts and re-checks. Oracle passed, confirming correctness.

The agent gathered all three diagnostic facts (cluster setting version, binary version, preserve_downgrade_option) up front in one batched command, reasoned about which value was the right one to record, checked whether the ConfigMap already existed before acting, and then created it — a clean diagnose-then-act sequence with no backtracking or wasted turns (4 total turns).

Used read-only kubectl exec/get calls for diagnosis and a single scoped kubectl create configmap --from-literal for the mutation — least-privilege, targeted commands with no broad or risky flags, matching the API activity log (1 mutation, 11 reads).
Exactly one mutation (creating the ConfigMap with the single required key), no unrelated changes or collateral churn — the minimal possible solution to the stated task.

summary: all stages passed and the regression sweep is clean -> 99.2.
